does: This paper systematically evaluates the security vulnerabilities of large language model-based multi-agent systems against adversarial attacks, specifically investigating how model scale influences susceptibility to prompt injection and jailbreaking within linear workflows. The authors conduct controlled experiments across different model sizes using two open-weight families on HumanEval, measuring the performance degradation when agents are compromised by malicious instructions. They discover a compliance-correction symmetry where larger models exhibit higher fidelity to adversarial prompts, causing performance drops up to 53.7 percentage points in uncorrected pipelines. Critically, they demonstrate that appending a lightweight Fixer agent at the end of the workflow dramatically reduces this vulnerability to just 0.6 percentage points, showing that linear multi-agent architectures can maintain resilience against compromise through simple correction mechanisms.
connects: This work builds on growing literature examining adversarial robustness in language models and multi-agent systems, extending beyond single-model attack research to study how collaborative structures affect system-level security. It responds to concerns about prompt injection and jailbreaking vulnerabilities in deployed LLM systems by investigating the understudied interaction between model scaling and adversarial resilience in agent workflows. The paper relates to research on model interpretability and behavioral control at scale, as well as work on defensive mechanisms in LLM systems. It connects to earlier findings about scaling laws and emergent capabilities, showing how larger models' improved instruction-following paradoxically increases their susceptibility to malicious directives, while also building on research suggesting that ensemble or multi-stage approaches can provide robustness benefits.
enables: This work opens directions for designing inherently resilient multi-agent architectures that balance performance, scalability, and security without requiring expensive retraining or alignment procedures. It suggests that lightweight correction stages represent a practical defense mechanism scalable to larger models and more complex workflows. The compliance-correction symmetry finding could inform future research on selective compliance mechanisms that allow agents to distinguish legitimate from adversarial instructions, as well as theoretical work on fundamental tradeoffs between capability and control in scaled systems. Results hint at opportunities for developing more sophisticated correction mechanisms, exploring non-linear workflow topologies for additional resilience, and understanding whether similar lightweight defenses apply to other multi-agent collaboration patterns, potentially advancing practical deployment of multi-agent systems in high-stakes environments.
